blocks.text: Suppose two persons have a dispute and enter into litigation, and the judges decide between them, declaring one to be in the right and the other to be in the wrong.

Chs 24–25: Laws promoting social harmony. – : This section (ch 24), like the previous one (ch 23), begins with a restriction on marriage and concludes stipulating care for those in need (note 22.30 = 23.1 Heb).
If the one in the wrong deserves to be flogged, the judge shall make that person lie down and be beaten in his presence with the number of lashes proportionate to the offense.
2 : , under the judge’s direct supervision, “by [his] count” (the more likely rendering of NRSV ).
Forty lashes may be given but not more; if more lashes than these are given, your neighbor will be degraded in your sight.
25.1–3 : A double restriction upon judicial flogging, also employed for discipline in nonjudicial contexts (Ex 21.20; Prov 10.13; 26.3; 1 Kings 12.14).

3 : , a definitive restriction for which there is no parallel in Near Eastern law; the stipulate floggings of five to one hundred lashes ( §B 7–10). , the criminal, despite his judicial status, retains human dignity.
You shall not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain.
4 : For similar humane treatment of animals, see 22.6–7; Prov 12.10.

When brothers reside together, and one of them dies and has no son, the wife of the deceased shall not be married outside the family to a stranger. Her husband’s brother shall go in to her, taking her in marriage, and performing the duty of a husband’s brother to her,

5 : : in joint tenancy on patrimonial land not yet subdivided among them as heirs. The law addresses the contingency of a father who dies leaving sons who are not yet ready to set up their own independent households. In such circumstances, the patrimony would be managed jointly until the land could be properly divided. However, should one of the heirs die childless prior to this division of property, he and his line would never inherit his share; it would be subsumed into the others’ shares. Thus, the purpose of the levirate law was to “build up the house” of the deceased son (v. 10), to ensure he had an heir to inherit his fair share of the jointly held property. , possibly the brother’s death provided an exception to the incest prohibition of marrying a sister‐in‐law (cf. the prohibition in the later Lev 18.16; 20.21).
and the firstborn whom she bears shall succeed to the name of the deceased brother, so that his name may not be blotted out of Israel.
6 : of inheritance rights; cf. Gen 38.8.

But if the man has no desire to marry his brother’s widow, then his brother’s widow shall go up to the elders at the gate and say, “My husband’s brother refuses to perpetuate his brother’s name in Israel; he will not perform the duty of a husband’s brother to me.”
Then the elders of his town shall summon him and speak to him. If he persists, saying, “I have no desire to marry her,”

then his brother’s wife shall go up to him in the presence of the elders, pull his sandal off his foot, spit in his face, and declare, “This is what is done to the man who does not build up his brother’s house.”
9 : The intent of the ceremony is public shaming for his dereliction of duty.
Throughout Israel his family shall be known as “the house of him whose sandal was pulled off.”

5–10 : Biblical, Near Eastern, and Roman inheritance law assigned special responsibilities to the “husband’s brother” (vv. 5–7), for which Hebrew had a special term (cf. Latin “levir,” hence “levirate marriage”). See variant versions of this law in Gen 38.8 and Ruth 4.5–6.

10 : , lit., “called by the name of,” thus explicitly applying a form of retributive justice (19.19n.). For refusing to build up the deceased’s “name” (vv. 6–7) and “house” (v. 9), the brother’s own is stigmatized by its new name.
If men get into a fight with one another, and the wife of one intervenes to rescue her husband from the grip of his opponent by reaching out and seizing his genitals,
you shall cut off her hand; show no pity.

11–12 : An additional law dealing with threats to reproduction (cf. Ex 21.22–25). Physical mutilation (characteristic in the ) is nowhere else prescribed in the Bible, except in the general formula for talion (19.21; Ex 21.23–24; Lev 24.19–20). That rationale does not apply here, however, since there is no symmetry between injury and punishment. The issue may rather be the perceived insult to dignity (cf. §195).
You shall not have in your bag two kinds of weights, large and small.
You shall not have in your house two kinds of measures, large and small.
14 : By fraudulently using two different sets of counterweights—small ones to sell grain but large ones to purchase it—a merchant could turn a tidy profit.
You shall have only a full and honest weight; you shall have only a full and honest measure, so that your days may be long in the land that the Lord your God is giving you.
For all who do such things, all who act dishonestly, are abhorrent to the Lord your God.
Remember what Amalek did to you on your journey out of Egypt,
how he attacked you on the way, when you were faint and weary, and struck down all who lagged behind you; he did not fear God.
18 : These details are not found in Ex 17.8–16; they may have been supplied by the Deuteronomic author in order to justify the extirpation of Amalek (v. 19; cf. Ex 17.14; 1 Sam 15.2–3).

Therefore when the Lord your God has given you rest from all your enemies on every hand, in the land that the Lord your God is giving you as an inheritance to possess, you shall blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven; do not forget.
4.44–28.68: The second discourse of Moses.

12.1–26.15: The legal corpus: Deuteronomy’s transformation of earlier Israelite religion. Just as Deuteronomy provides a significant reinterpretation of earlier narrative traditions about the nation’s past (see 1.19–46n; 4.1–40n; 5.1–33n; 7.9–11n; etc.), so does it here advance a major new understanding of the nation’s religious requirements and legal traditions.

21.10–25.19: Miscellaneous civil and family laws. The following laws are concerned with family, civil, and ethical issues. Laws to extend legal protection to women when they would otherwise be disenfranchised concern female captives (21.10–14), property rights of the less‐favored wife (21.15–17), and false charges of infidelity (22.13–19).
23.15–25.19: The heightened moral responsibilities of the covenant community.
17–19 : Builds upon Ex 17.8–16, in which the Amalekites, a fierce desert tribe, attacked Israel (cf. Ps 83.4–8).
